data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor Earnings Preview: Despite Risks And Headwinds, Valuation Remains Attractive
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor is one of the best-managed companies and important companies in the world. - Warren Buffett

We have been admirers of Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) even before the market caught AI fever. We agree with Warren Buffett that it is probably one of the most important companies in the world, and probably one of the most interesting as well. Unfortunately, there is some idiosyncratic risk, which Buffett made clear when he expressed admiration for Taiwan Semiconductor, but still sold Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.A)(BRK.B) multi-billion dollar position in TSMC because "he did not like the location".

While we acknowledge the risk highlighted by Buffett, we believe a small position in TSMC can still be justified if the valuation is right for investors with a higher risk tolerance. It is important to mention that should TSMC's chip production be disrupted, the effect would also be catastrophic for many of its customers such as NVIDIA (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL).

It will be interesting to listen to the upcoming earnings call on April 17th, in particular management's take on the potential impact from tariffs and the increased risk of a recession in the U.S. In the meantime, as TSMC reports monthly sales we already know that Q1 revenue slightly exceeded analyst estimates. Revenue of NT$839.25 billion, or ~$25.6 billion, was an increase of roughly 41.6% year-over-year. This is impressive, although some could be pull-forward demand as a result of tariff uncertainty. We will have to wait until the earnings report to see if the full-year sales target is revised given recent developments, and if a weakening global outlook for chip demand results in a revised capital expenditures plan.

Technology Leader

While semiconductor manufacturing equipment makers like ASML (ASML) get a lot of credit for recent chip advances, and rightly so, optimizing the use of these machines is a technical feat as well. In some ways, companies like ASML and Applied Materials (AMAT) are the oven and kitchen utensils manufacturers, while TSMC is the Master Chef that figures out the optimal way to use these tools to produce the best results.

Given the computing power and energy efficiency benefits from smaller transistors, it is no surprise that many customers want their chips manufactured on the leading edge, which currently means 5nm and 3nm semiconductor nodes, for which there appears to be an insatiable demand. Importantly, Taiwan Semiconductor is not resting on its laurels, instead it is working to maintain or widen its lead over the competition. For example, during the Q4 earnings call, management highlighted that they are on track to start producing the 2nm (N2) node in the second half of 2025. This scale down is expected to result in a more than 10x speed improvement at the same power, or a 20% to 30% power improvement at constant speed. This should be particularly attractive for AI data center chips, which are already struggling to procure enough electricity.

Finally, I will talk about N2 and A16 introduction. Our 2 nanometer and A16 technologies lead the industry in addressing the insatiable need for energy-efficient computing, and almost all the innovators are working with TSMC. We expect a number of the new tapeout for 2 nanometer technology in the first two years to be higher than both 3 nanometer and 5 nanometer in their first two years, fueled by both smartphone and HPC applications. N2 will deliver full-node performance and power benefit with 10 to 15 speed improvement at the same power or 20% to 30% power improvement at the same speed, and more than 15% chip density increase as compared with the N3E. N2 is well on track for volume production in second half of 2025 as scheduled, with a ramp profile similar to N3.

There is very strong demand for the leading nodes, as can be seen from the rapid increase in their contribution to Taiwan Semiconductor's revenue. For example, the 3nm node accounted for ~6% of the company's revenue in 2023 and roughly tripled to ~18% in 2024. Almost three quarters of revenue is attributed to nodes at 7nm and below.

Smaller transistors is not the only research and development focus for Taiwan Semiconductor. It has delivered impressive innovations in the field of inter-chip stacking technology. This allows for much better integrated systems in a single chip, which has multiple benefits: higher density, faster speeds, and lower power consumption. Taiwan Semiconductor's TSMC-SoIC™ technology is a 3D inter-chip stacking technology that enables integration of chiplets with multi-layers, multi-chip sizes, and multi-functions. This way, customers can receive complete Systems on a Chip (SoC) that improve performance and reduce costs and time to market. The company estimates this can result in roughly 10x speed performance, 190x higher bandwidth, and a 20x improvement in energy efficiency compared to traditional 3D ICs.

Perhaps unsurprisingly, high-performance computing (HPC) customers like NVIDIA and AMD (AMD), that derive enormous benefits from having their chips manufactured using cutting-edge technology, represented slightly more than half of revenue in 2024. HPC was also the platform that saw the largest year-over-year growth rate in 2024.

Unassailable Competitive Moat

Through a combination of factors, we believe Taiwan Semiconductor has built a very strong competitive moat. These include its vast economies of scale, technological leadership, high customer switching costs, and superb execution skills.

Competitors like Samsung Foundry have frequently had yield issues, and in some cases directly competes with potential customers. Similarly, Intel has experienced significant delays in its technology roadmap, and in some cases directly competes with potential customers. The competitive moat is reflected in the strong financials, including a gross profit margin approaching 60%, operating margins of almost 50%, and Returns on Invested Capital (ROIC) well above 20%. For comparison, Samsung's ROIC is ~8.5% while Intel's ROIC is currently negative.

Financials

Taiwan Semiconductor reports its monthly revenue, where we can see there has been a year-over-year growth acceleration. It is likely that part of the reason can be attributed to some customers trying to get chips imported before any potential tariff impact. As previously mentioned, revenue for the first quarter is slightly higher than expected by analysts, which is why we expect solid earnings results. What will be perhaps more important to how shares react will be guidance for the rest of the year.

Taking a longer-term view, it is clear Taiwan Semiconductor has delivered impressive growth over the past decade, despite some cyclicality. Its revenue has more than tripled, while Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) has only grown revenue by ~22%, and Intel has seen negative revenue growth.

Balance Sheet

Despite the high capital intensity that goes along with chip manufacturing, Taiwan Semiconductor has managed to keep a rock-solid balance sheet. It has a net cash position, and relied mostly on internally generated funds to finance its growth.

This has resulted in strong investment grade credit ratings, with S&P Global (SPGI) giving assigning 'AA-', while Moody's (MCO) gives the company an 'Aa3' rating. Taiwan Semiconductor has also proven disciplined when it comes to M&A.

Given its strong profitability and cash generation, Taiwan Semiconductor has actually been increasing its net cash position. This is impressive considering the significant capital expenditures and research and development investments the company has been making.

U.S.-China Economic Decoupling

Of course, the big risk on many investors' minds is the one Warren Buffett alluded to: the company's geographical location. While we still view a Chinese military invasion of Taiwan as a relatively low probability risk, there is no doubt the impact would be enormous. Unfortunately, the trade war, which is further decoupling the U.S. and Chinese economies, increases this risk, as China has now less to lose from potential economic sanctions.

This risk is difficult to quantify, and it is completely understandable that more risk-averse investors prefer to avoid investing in the company. That said, it is important to note that the potential impact on Taiwan Semiconductor's customers could also be massive, as they are extremely dependent on the company for their chips. Taiwan Semiconductor is mitigating the potential risk by opening fabs in the U.S., Europe, and Japan. Still, for the foreseeable future, most of its capacity, especially for leading nodes, will remain in Taiwan.

Future Outlook

From the monthly revenue reports, we know Taiwan Semiconductor is expected to report revenue that exceeds analyst expectations. Gross margins are expected to be slightly lower compared to Q42024, with the company warning that Q1 gross margin would see a decline of roughly 100 basis points due to ramp-up costs associated with N2 and its advanced packaging capabilities.

Looking further ahead, there are both headwinds and tailwinds to consider. The outlook for AI chips growth remains strong, with CEO C.C. Wei predicting an astounding mid-40% compound annual growth rates (CAGR) in the AI space alone over the next five years, and 20% revenue growth for all platforms:

Underpinned by our technology leadership and broad customer base, we now forecast the revenue growth from AI accelerators to approach a mid-40% CAGR for the five-year period, starting off the already higher base of 2024. We expect AI accelerators to be the strongest driver of our HPC platform growth and the largest contributor in terms of our overall incremental revenue growth in the next several years.

[...]

For the five-year period starting from 2024, we expect our long-term revenue growth to approach a 20% CAGR in U.S. dollar term, fueled by all four of our growth platforms which are smartphone, HPC, IoT and automotive.

We believe AI demand will continue to grow, but we are somewhat skeptical of such an elevated CAGR being sustainable for that long. For example, Microsoft (MSFT) is already rethinking its data center needs in various markets. Still, even if demand is reduced we believe that cancelled orders could potentially be replaced with new customers jumping at the opportunity to move from other foundries to work with Taiwan Semiconductor to benefit from the advantages of having chips manufactured with its cutting-edge technology.

Taiwan Semiconductor is certainly displaying confidence that customer demand will remain strong, as it continues with aggressive factory expansions, with high volume production expected soon in Arizona and Japan. Analysts appear optimistic as well, with Taiwan Semiconductor having more upward earnings and revenue revisions in the past three months, compared to downward revisions.

Valuation

If Taiwan Semiconductor and ASML were based in the U.S., we believe we would be talking about the magnificent nine, instead of the magnificent seven. In terms of growth, profit margins, and returns on invested capital, Taiwan Semiconductor is definitely one of the top companies in the world. Unlike most of the magnificent seven, Taiwan Semiconductor has a very reasonable valuation with a GAAP P/E (TTM) multiple of only ~17x, a PEG ratio of ~0.45x, and a net cash position equivalent to ~5% of its market capitalization.

Compared to its two most important customers, Apple and NVIDIA, and its most important supplier, ASML, Taiwan Semiconductor's valuation stands out as significantly cheaper.

Based on forward earnings estimated, shares appear even more undervalued, with the forward Price/Earnings multiple for FY2026 currently below 13x.

Risks

It is probably safe to say that at this point nobody really knows where tariffs will end up, as we have seen new developments and announcements almost daily the last few weeks. While we believe tariffs will likely be an overall negative for TSMC, there could be some hidden benefits. For example, it appears China will exempt U.S. chips from its retaliatory tariffs if they were manufactured outside the country. This could benefit fabless companies like AMD, NVIDIA, and Qualcomm, to the detriment of companies like Intel (INTC) and Texas Instruments (TXN).

The biggest risk remains potential Chinese military action on Taiwan. Still, there are other important risks to consider, including potential operational risks, including the N2 ramp and executing on its global fab expansion.

Tariffs, regulatory changes, and exchange rates can also present potential headwinds. Perhaps more worrying, U.S. consumers have been showing increasing signs of weakness, which could reduce discretionary purchases like smartphones and computers. There are many signs pointing to consumer weakness, including plunging consumer sentiment, and more credit card users opting to make only the minimum payments.

Conclusion

There is no doubt Taiwan Semiconductor is a wonderful company trading at a very attractive price. However, this comes with geopolitical risk, which is probably rising as the U.S. and Chinese economies decouple as a result of the escalating trade war. Still, should TSMC's factories be shut-off the effect would have a massive impact on the entire global economy, including many U.S. technology giants like NVIDIA and Apple.

We believe that, depending on each investor's risk tolerance, a position can be justified or not. While we fully acknowledge the risk that prompted Warren Buffett to sell, it is extremely difficult to find companies with the kind of fundamentals that Taiwan Semiconductor has, including potential annual revenue growth above 20%, and a very wide competitive moat, trading at less than 20 times earnings.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Braze: AI Acquisition Helps, But Avoid During Downturn
data.description:
Braze (BRZE) is a customer engagement platform that helps companies better interact with their customers. The company offers messaging solutions across several channels, such as email, SMS, and push notifications. Braze integrates with several data sources and uses this to help orchestrate marketing campaigns.

Even though the stock has been under a lot of pressure so far in 2025, I believe the path forward is still lower. Revenue growth is on a decelerating trend and profits are still minimal. Plus, with the prospects of a potential slowdown in consumer confidence, companies may opt to reduce their marketing spend, which would directly impact Braze.

Yes, valuation could be considered attractive at face value, but potential macro headwinds may cause growth to slow even more than expected. If that were the case, then valuation could see even more downside.

For now, I believe investors are best waiting for a better entry point. While difficult to time the market, this stock seems to be pricing in too much optimism given current market conditions.

Financial Update

During the company’s recent Q4 report, revenue grew 22.5% YOY to $160.4 million, coming in ~$4.7 million above expectations. Subscription revenue, which is the vast majority (~96%) of revenue, grew 22.2% YOY, slightly below the total revenue growth.

At face value, 22.5% revenue growth is very strong. However, the company’s growth has come under pressure over the past several quarters. Two years ago, Braze was growing over 40% YOY. That growth has since been nearly cut in half.

One of the biggest reasons behind this deceleration is increased customer churn. Management has talked about customers buying excess amount of subscriptions during the COVID-fueled growth era. As these contracts came up for renewal, many customers were reducing how much spend they were having with Braze.

Dollar-based net retention is a great metric that shows existing customer spending trends. For example, the 111% reported in Q4 means that existing customers spent 111% of the year-ago base, or 11% YOY growth.

This metric has been on a steady decline, for the reasons I noted above. With revenue trends continuing to decelerate, one should assume that the dollar-based net retention metric also comes under pressure.

With revenue having been under pressure, many investors would naturally look for a lift in profitability. Although profits have been improving at Braze, margins are still low.

During the most recent fiscal year, non-GAAP operating income was $0.0 million, or a breakeven 0% margin. Free cash flow for the year was $19.6 million, or ~3.3% margin. Improving profitability is something the company has talked a lot about in recent quarters, and yes, there is progress being made. However, the pace of profit improvements may be below what investors would like to see.

BRZE’s guidance for FY ’26 includes revenue of $686-691 million, or ~16% YOY growth. This once again assumes another year of revenue deceleration. Non-GAAP operating income of $25.5-29.5 million demonstrates an improved ~4% operating margin, and while this is nice to see, it remains low in absolute terms.

Braze also announced an agreement to acquire OfferFit, a company that provides AI-decisioning for personalization in CRM marketing. The $325 million purchase price is a large sum to pay for a company with a current market cap ~$3 billion. In addition, while this acquisition presents an opportunity to accelerate revenue growth, it comes at the cost of margin dilution.

While we expect to remain operating income positive for the year, inclusive of the proposed acquisition of OfferFit, the dilutive impact in fiscal year 2026 is likely to result in a temporary departure from the framework, with a return to a more meaningful year-over-year operating income expansion next year.

With the current macro environment being highly uncertain, there is potential that growth estimates will need to come down. Braze has already suggested that margins will be low once the OfferFit acquisition closes, so it appears the next revision of estimates will be low.

More Cautious Consumer Presents Headwinds

The recent headlines around potential tariff introductions and general concern about entering into a slower period of growth has weighed on the market for the past week. Among the market volatility, investors have started to take a “risk-off” approach, or sell stocks that are more exposed to macro volatility.

One area of enterprise spending that would likely slow down during a prolonged period of economic uncertainty would be marketing. This is frequently the easiest expense item that companies can flex up/down based on the buying environment. Are consumers more likely to have strong purchasing power? Then increase marketing efforts. Are consumers more likely to curtail their spending? Then slow down marketing efforts.

The Morning Consult offers a real-time indicator of consumer confidence, as seen in the chart above. While consumer confidence remains well above the low-point seen during COVID and the macro fears in 2022, the recent retracement in confidence is worrying.

Does this mean that companies will immediately slow down their marketing campaigns? Probably not. However, the negative trend may give companies more hesitation to increase their marketing efforts. And if lower consumer confidence in turn results in less spending, then history would suggest that marketing budgets, including the money spent on Braze’s solutions, may be at risk.

According to McKinsey & Company, one of their studies from December 2022 found that marketing budgets are an early expense item to be cut. This 2022 study was great timing given the weaker consumer confidence, as seen in the chart above.

With the economy still sending mixed signals, it’s no great surprise that companies are feeling skittish and cutting costs. Unfortunately, the marketing budget is often the first to go. In December 2022, we surveyed nearly three dozen chief marketing officers (CMOs) of major North American consumer companies. On average, they told us that their company boards demanded an 8 percent reduction in marketing expenditures over the previous 12 months. In some cases, marketing budgets were cut by as much as 10 to 20 percent. One large public company even slashed its marketing budget by more than 20 percent.

While I am not advocating that there are major red flags in terms of marketing budgets slowing down, but it is important to note the correlation between the 2022 slowdown and ensuing marketing cuts.

Valuation

Especially during volatile markets, software and technology companies can be nearly impossible to value. Many investors use the Rule of 40 score, or the sum of revenue growth and operating margin, to determine the relative attractiveness of a company.

Given BRZE’s guidance assumes a score of just <20, the stock likely deserves a discounted valuation.

During the prosperous periods of growth for Braze, the stock was trading at over 10x revenue. Currently, valuation stands at ~4.2x revenue.

As a rule of thumb, companies with a Rule of 40 score, or higher, often trade at >6x revenue, with leading companies trading over 10x. Some of the lower performing companies can trade at multiples as low as 2x revenue.

While I am not advocating that BRZE should be trading at 2x revenue, the decelerating revenue trends and low levels of profitability warrant a discounted multiple. Even at ~4.2x revenue, there seems to be more risk of the multiple coming down, especially if revenue growth becomes even more pressured this year due to macro challenges.

For now, I believe investors should avoid Braze and wait for improving macro signals. Buying a company with decelerating growth trends and a murky macro is always a difficult buy.

Risks

One of the biggest risks to my negative thesis is if consumer spending remains strong. If this holds true, then companies are likely to continue their marketing spend at normal levels, which would mean no pressure on Braze’s business. If revenue growth and profitability were to improve, the stock would likely experience a higher valuation, thus a higher stock price.

Additionally, companies often use a good deal of different marketing software vendors, with Braze being just one of them. While Braze includes many of the Fortune 1000 companies as their customers, competition from traditional vendors, such as Salesforce and Adobe, remains strong. Increased competition could result in customer churn.

data.title: Alphabet: I'd Load Up At $130
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL), better known as ‘Google,’ is one of the cheapest Magnificent 7 stocks on the market today. Trading at just 20 times earnings, it has multiples more typical of Chinese tech stocks than U.S. tech stocks. This is a remarkable fact because Google has a strong brand, a fast-growing cloud business, several products with over a billion users, and many other advantages. You’d think that a company with all this going for it would command a premium price, but Google is actually rather cheap.

One reason why Google stock is cheap right now is because it has a lot of capital expenditure [CAPEX] planned for this year. Like most big tech companies, Google is investing heavily in generative AI, and that entails a lot of spending. The company has guided for $75 billion in CAPEX in 2025, a full $17 billion more than Wall Street expected before the last earnings release. The big jump in expenses is a risk factor in itself; also, it isn’t likely that Google is turning profits on its generative AI offerings apart from those part of its cloud services segment: competitors OpenAI and Meta Platforms (META) do not expect their gen AI investments to become profitable for many years.

All of this is set against the backdrop of the current administration and its capricious economic policies. Donald Trump recently threatened to hit Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) with a 100% tariff if it doesn’t build a plant in the United States. The tax was never actually enacted, but the fact that Trump is threatening it while having enacted similar taxes just recently, suggests that the risk is a real one. If Trump did put a 100% tax on TSMC chips, it would materially impact Google, as Google depends on TSMC for manufacturing, as do most big tech companies. So the risk here is very real.

It’s for this reason that investors ought to demand a discount to Google’s recent valuation. The level of uncertainty in this market is off the charts, and you can’t rule out the possibility that another round of tariffs will make Google’s AI CAPEX even more costly.

At the same time, the company does have many things going for it. That’s undeniable. It has high margins, a great growth track record, a strong brand, and capable leadership. These are real assets. However, in this environment of heightened risk, investors ought to demand cheaper asset prices relative to earnings and cash flows, because many of the variables impacting valuation are completely unpredictable–I mean more unpredictable than usual!

When I last covered Google stock, I rated it a hold because its valuation had gotten stretched–this was last year when the price was at $202. In the article, I mentioned that I had held the stock before but had sold it near $170. Given that Google stock is cheaper than $170 now, and I’m still holding it, my relatively bearish tone might sound surprising. However, the level of risk impacting markets is simply much higher now than it was last year. Accordingly, I am maintaining my GOOG rating at hold, despite the stock having declined in price substantially since I last covered it.

Fundamentals

According to Seeking Alpha Quant, Google stock boasts the following fundamental metrics:

  • $8.04 in diluted EPS.

  • $24 in tangible book value [BV] per share.

  • $5.91 in free cash flow [FCF] per share.

Since 2021, Google’s earnings and BV have grown, while its FCF has flatlined. The sluggish FCF performance is a concern. If you look at Google’s cash flow statement, you can clearly see that its capital expenditures have been rising significantly since 2021. Much of this CAPEX spend is due to generative AI. It could well be that the company will have elevated CAPEX indefinitely due to needing more and more NVIDIA (NVDA) chips each year in order to stay ahead of competitors. In that scenario, the company’s future growth and margins will not be as good as those seen in the past. On the other hand, there is also the possibility that viable competitors to NVIDIA will emerge, and make running AI cheaper. In that scenario, Google has a very bright future ahead of it, but it’s hard to say which of these two scenarios will actually play out. Accordingly, it makes sense to want a lower earnings/FCF multiple on GOOG stock than the one the stock has historically had. Costs appear to be rising.

Market Risk and Uncertainty

Another risk factor for Google–and one that relates to the previous one discussed–is the rising uncertainty in the markets as a whole. Donald Trump’s tariff policies appear to be quite costly to companies on the surface of things, and they are extremely unpredictable. In the span of a week, we heard a report that Trump was considering a 90-day pause on tariffs, followed by a White House denial of the report, followed by an official White House announcement that the 90-day pause was going ahead. It’s simply not possible to predict sequences of events this volatile, and so, investors can only expect the unexpected. In an environment where relevant variables can’t be predicted, investors ought to demand a cheaper price.

Some of the unpredictable tariff impacts mentioned above are relevant to Google’s business. Trump has threatened to tariff TSMC semiconductors at 100%--that would dramatically ramp up Google’s expenses because Google buys many chips from TSMC. He has also threatened overall tariffs on Vietnam and China, where Pixel phones are manufactured. The tariff on China is still in effect, as it wasn’t reversed in Wednesday’s 90-day tariff pause. So there are parts of Google’s business that may be facing tariffs right now, and others that could face them in the future. So the market risks discussed in the previous paragraphs are also company-specific risks to Google.

Valuing Google

Looking at everything written above, there is a case to be made that Google is a very strong company facing increased risk and uncertainty. In light of this, how do we value the stock?

First, let’s look at the stock’s multiples, courtesy of Seeking Alpha Quant.

As you can see, Google trades at about 20 times earnings. This is below average for the Magnificent 7 and for the NASDAQ-100–that is a definite positive. However, this is an environment of far greater risk than that observed in the past. The fact that Google is cheaper than its peer group may not be enough. Investors may demand earnings multiples closer to 15 or even 10 in order to account for the uncertainty.

Now, let’s look at Google’s cash flows and earnings.

According to Seeking Alpha Quant, Google had $5.91 in FCF per share and $8.13 in EPS in the TTM period. Given the uncertainty present, we cannot forecast any near term growth. What we know is that $5.91 discounted at 5% is worth $118.20 and $8.13 discounted at 5% is $162.60. The average of the two is $140. The reason I average two metrics here is because it’s uncertain for how long Google’s elevated CAPEX will remain–it affects FCF immediately but EPS only gradually through depreciation. So I think $140 is the better measure to go with here: it accounts for the uncertainty about the CAPEX schedule. So $140 is the upper limit of what I’d pay for Google stock today. However, in this environment, it pays to err on the side of caution, so $130 is where I would, in fact, buy Google.

The Bottom-Line

We are in an environment of elevated uncertainty today. Nobody knows where the tariff cards will fall, and there are other risk factors to consider too. My conservative no-growth Google model technically spits out $140 as the fair value estimate for the stock, and I think investors would do fine over the very long term buying at that level. But personally, I’d err on the side of caution and pay $130.

data.title: Oakmark Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio activity

The Fund entered the new year maintaining a modest overweight position to corporate credit and securitized debt and was underweight U.S. Treasurys versus its benchmark. That said, the majority of the Fund’s securitized position is in highly-rated agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), and the corporate holdings are high credit quality with less than 20% in below investment grade. The Fund’s duration is approximately in-line with the benchmark at around six years and its yield-to-worst ended the first quarter at 5.4% with a current yield of 5.38%.

Excess returns for corporate credit in the first quarter were negative as investment grade corporate index spreads widened by 14 basis points. This weighed on performance as the Fund was moderately overweight in corporate credit versus its benchmark, while the Fund’s overweight to securitized debt added to performance given solid returns for that sector.

The Fund’s average weight to corporate credit was approximately 34.5% during the quarter versus about 24.2% for the benchmark. The Fund maintained an average overweight to securitized credit of roughly 11.7% versus about 1.2% for the benchmark. Security selection within corporate credit was slightly negative during the quarter and was partially offset by positive security selection in securitized debt.

Positioning and outlook

As mentioned in our first-quarter 2025 fixed income commentary, Today’s uncertainty gets priced into the market, the uncertainty driven by the recently announced tariffs and related news headlines has started to affect the fixed income market. For value investors like us, lowering prices signal emerging opportunity.

The repricing hasn’t been dramatic, but it matters. It’s how value in credit starts to rebuild. High yield (Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index) has widened to +375 as of this writing—almost 100 basis points off the tights (+281 on February 18, 2025) or a move of roughly 33%. Investment-grade BBB credit (Bloomberg U.S. BBB Corporate Index)—where most investment grade issuance sits—has widened to around +120, 25 basis points wider and 27% off the lows. Since the global financial crisis, spreads for high yields have ranged between 300–650 basis points, and spreads for investment grade have ranged between 100–200 for investment grade 84% of the time since March 20101. So, the recent shifts represent meaningful resets. They have the potential to generate not just total return, but also excess return, for long-term, patient capital.

In parts of high yield and BBB investment grade, spreads are finally starting to reflect real risks: business model fragility, capital structure leverage, and refinancing gaps. Securitized credit is repricing, too, especially in deals tied to consumer credit and commercial real estate where stress is more visible. These aren’t broad calls. We believe this market still rewards real credit work, not index exposure. But for the first time in a while, dislocations are surfacing that meet Oakmark’s bar for both return potential and margin of safety.

Finding opportunity in dislocations

One such dislocation emerged in auto asset-backed securities (auto ABS). We bought a newly issued BBB-rated tranche at a credit spread of 190 basis points, almost double what the market would have priced that level of risk even three weeks earlier. Such a large move was purely technical and disconnected from our view of the investment’s fundamental risk. In fact, the underlying auto loans would have to experience cumulative losses above 40% for our investment to see any write-down, while that type of loan pool typically exhibits losses below 20%. In our opinion, that healthy margin of safety against losses, combined with a rapid cheapening of credit spreads, created the kind of opportunity that fits our value investment thesis.

We also added exposure in Insulet Corp. (PODD) by participating in its inaugural senior unsecured bond deal used to refinance part of its 2026 convertible notes. This marked a meaningful evolution in PODD’s capital structure—from a growth-phase mix of converts and loans to something more befitting its scale and cash flow profile. We see it as a high-BB credit, anchored by a market leadership position in insulin delivery, strong free cash flow, and modest, declining leverage near 2.5x. Price talk came in at 7%, roughly 50 basis points wide of comparable high-quality healthcare credits–and 50 basis points wider than it would have been priced earlier in March before valuations in credit began to reset. That kind of mispricing doesn’t just offer near-term yield. It sets up longer term potential upside as our upgrade thesis plays out. These are still selective opportunities, not a broad-based buying spree. But the key difference is that we’re finally being paid to take risk. After a long period of restraint, we believe it’s time to start leaning in—carefully, selectively, and always with our bottom-up framework in mind.

Still early days

My goal isn’t to signal the “all-in” on buying fixed income risk today. If tariffs of the magnitude that have been recently discussed are fully enacted and sustained, we believe there will be plenty of opportunities to buy at even lower prices than today, especially in more cyclical corners of the market. Credit markets today aren’t broadly cheap, but they’re far more interesting than they were just a quarter ago. Spread widening has opened isolated entry points that finally offer fair compensation for real risk—especially in companies with reliable management teams and revenue streams. The reset likely isn’t complete and may even be in the early stages, but it’s in motion.

Our positioning today

With that in mind, our portfolio positioning reflects a cautious optimism. We’ve begun selectively increasing exposure to credit risk in areas where spreads offer meaningful compensation. That includes certain segments of high yield, BBB-rated corporates, and asset-backed securities with conservative structures and favorable technicals. At the same time, we’ve maintained strong liquidity and continue to hold a healthy allocation of U.S. Treasurys and short-duration instruments—both for defense and optionality. The opportunity set is growing, but patience remains a core part of our process as we assess further possible disruptions in our companies’ earnings streams.

How much disruption—and ultimately what gets priced into the market—will depend in large part on macro policy. At Oakmark, we don’t try to predict how these events will play out. We leave that to the few PhDs who claim they can. But we don’t ignore it either. Tariffs are real. They have the potential to shift earnings and raise default risk.

We do spend time focusing on how the companies in our portfolio can absorb that reset—through cash flow, balance sheet strength, and liquidity. That’s the key distinction. As debt investors, we don’t need to predict the exact outcome. But we do need a strong sense of the range—especially the downside—and the catalysts that could take us there.

Rather than betting on discrete macro outcomes, we focus on building portfolios of companies led by management teams equipped to navigate a wide range of scenarios. While others try to forecast the next macro squall, hoping to steer around it, we’re doing something different: picking what we believe are the right captain and the right boat. We want leadership that has proven it can navigate calm seas and rough waters alike and businesses that are built to absorb volatility, not avoid it. If we trust the captain and know the vessel is sound, we don’t need to predict every storm. We just need to stay on course.

That means we position our portfolio to hold up in tightening or easing cycles, recession or recovery, inflation or deflation. And we focus on securities where we think the embedded risk premium compensates us for real uncertainty—not headlines. When dislocations emerge, we lean in where the downside is well understood, and the upside still reflects investor fear. That mindset kept us disciplined during years of aggressive pricing, and we believe it will now help us step back into a market that looks more compelling.

In short, the market feels different—not because uncertainty is new, but because it’s finally being acknowledged and priced. Tariffs may be the catalyst, and we are monitoring their effects on the market. But what is notable is the shift in sentiment from complacency to caution, from greed to fear. And that shift creates opportunity for disciplined value buyers.

Through it all, our process doesn’t change. We stay grounded in fundamentals. We don’t react to noise. We invest in management teams and capital structures built to handle a range of outcomes. And when we find mispriced risk with a wide margin of safety, we act.
