data.title: VNQ: Volatility Makes Commercial Real Estate A Buy
data.description:
The theme of the day at REITer’s Digest is investor psychology. Today, we will dive into a discussion about REITs, real estate, and the fundamentals of investing, highlighting thoughts from investors who are far smarter than yours truly. In the context of an increasingly volatile environment, we will begin to assemble the pieces of the discussion to create a more comprehensive forward strategy.

As real estate, and the rest of the market, experiences an unprecedented level of volatility, there may be opportunities for investors with a long-time horizon.

Throughout this article, we will focus on the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), which is one of the largest index funds investing in the commercial real estate sector. VNQ is a market capitalization weighted index fund that invests in some of the largest publicly traded real estate investment trusts in the world. In this article, we will provide an overview of the fund including performance, holdings, and the expenses. In the broader context of commercial real estate, we will discuss how VNQ has become victim to investor psychology and the whipsaw effect of repricing. While commercial real estate remains a low volatility, stable asset class, the share price of REITs around the world continues to bounce around with the ongoing announcement of novel trade policy.

ETF Overview

My prior coverage of VNQ was in February. Over this time, the fund itself has not changed significantly, but the market has. Those looking for more detail on the fund’s construction should refer to the prior coverage.

VNQ is still the largest ETF in the real estate sector. As usual, it feels prudent to explain that I use the term real estate sector because the fund includes investments in brokerage houses and advisors instead of real estate investment trusts exclusively. Historically, the main point that I have highlighted with VNQ is that the fund invests far and wide targeting both the good and the bad.

The fund is cost-efficient charging just 13 basis points of assets under management and is backed by one of the world's largest asset managers. The fund is liquid with tens of millions of shares trading daily and is even one of the few REIT exchange-traded funds to have options. By all accounts, those who are looking for a real estate ETF should consider VNQ.

That's my prior coverage of the fund, share prices have fallen by nearly 10%. Falling from more than $90 per share to just over $80 per share in two months has highlighted the extreme volatility in the marketplace.

So, let’s discuss.

Investor Psychology & Real Estate

As highlighted, the past several months have been a volatile time for the commercial real estate industry. While commercial real estate has historically been a low volatility, cash flow backed asset class, the modern era of pandemics, rising interest rates, and trade disputes has created an increasingly uncertain environment which has begun to shake a historically dependable asset class.

In fact, uncertainty has become so great around the commercial real estate sector that words and phrases like “freezing”, “locked up”, and “collapsing” have already come into play. Just today, Jared Toothman, the New York market leader for Lincoln Property Company, discussed paralysis within the commercial real estate sector.

People can't think past two weeks,” Lincoln Property Co. NYC Market Leader Jared Toothman said this morning at Bisnow’s New York Finance event.

But CRE has gotten awfully accustomed to uncertainty. Stubborn inflation, the future of the office, where valuations will reset, when interest rates will come down — those problems have had the industry holding a cautious stance for years.

At some point, CRE has to step away from macroeconomics and decide whether projects can stand on their own fundamentals. It’s a long, long game, after all.

“If you've got a partner who says a [deal] is pausing because of a week of Trump trading, then you probably don't want to be with that partner on a development deal for the next five years,” Toothman said. “So, it's good to see who's serious and who's not right now.

Toothman highlights, there is an extraordinary amount of weight placed on short-term thinking when it comes to making long-term decisions. In the development game, participants are forced to think months if not years ahead to gauge what the market will be willing to absorb and at what price it will purchase it. Like other periods of volatility such as the pandemic, capital partners within the development market have become increasingly unsteady and unwilling to deploy capital.

For participants in the commercial real estate industry, this means a significant slowdown in velocity and a reduced supply that will be anticipated to come online in coming years. With the priority placed on velocity in the commercial real estate market, slowing has little benefits for investors, developers, or even the people living in the United States.

Let us take a moment to step back and approach commercial real estate from a different angle. From my perspective, commercial real estate remains one of the most stable investments from a long-term perspective. Aside from the ebbs and flows of specific asset classes, I believe the merits of real estate will remain fundamentally unchanged over the coming decades. Without any real change to this thesis, observing participants begin to act somewhat irrationally makes me believe many need to hit the brakes.

Let’s shift gears and highlight a thought from Howard Marks on the world of private credit. Howard Marks became a legend in the bond industry through leadership in private lending. This means credit investing in debt that is illiquid or has no secondary market, such as corporate bonds and treasuries. This means that these bonds have no daily liquidity or mark to market, like commercial real estate. Marks has historically highlighted this lack of marking to market in his insights on private credit, such as Gimme Credit.

What about the lack of marking to market, and the resulting low level of volatility? It’s obviously unrealistic to think the value of private loans doesn’t fluctuate. But on the other hand, it seems many people consider the non-marking to market a plus, in that they can report that their investments didn’t go down much in a difficult environment. Private credit managers are supposed to mark their holdings to reality based on fundamentals, but that’s clearly less volatile (and less objective) than marking to a market. On the other hand, is it desirable that public asset prices reflect every up and down of investor psychology? Not marking to market may be unrealistic, but it may be welcome. (Investors in public securities could have the same experience if they refused to read the newspapers and tossed their brokerage statements in the drawer, but such behavior would be called irresponsible.)

The conclusion that Marks lays out could be one of the most important pieces of wisdom in a highly volatile market. Investors in public securities could perhaps save themselves the insanity of watching today's whipsaw market by simply ignoring daily pricing of REITs and focusing on the fundamental merits of the assets or REITs themselves through changes in quarterly cash flow. It is hard to believe that the asset values of many pieces of commercial real estate have declined as precipitously as VNQ’s share price over the past month.

However, herein lies a critical distinction between REITs and commercial real estate itself.

REITs vs. Real Estate

The world of REITs and commercial real estate has several critical differentiators. Foremost, the similarities are obvious. Typically, REITs hold commercial real estate, making them a scalable and efficient opportunity for the retail investor to access high-quality commercial real estate that costs millions of dollars. For many of us, it may be our only option to invest in these assets. However, that does not mean that investing in each has identical consequences.

Leaving aside the world of non-traded REITs which are valued at their net asset value as calculated by the fund and verified by auditors, the world of publicly traded REITs has little connection to the asset values.

This concept was described well by Chris Volk with Seeking Alpha Analyst Brad Thomas.

REIT investors are not buying into mutual funds of real estate. They're buying into real estate operating companies. The former presumes that returns will be derived from the buying and selling of real estate. The latter presumes that investor returns will be derived from corporate business model operating fundamentals.

NAV does have one benefit. Investors can estimate the break-up value of the real estate operating companies in which they invest. This is certainly a tried-and-true approach taken by activist shareholders. But remember to keep in mind that estimating asset values is only part of the game. You also must estimate the costs to eliminate or assume the liability stack.

Whereas public REIT values reflect the worth of their operating business models, non-traded REITs have traditionally issued and repurchased shares based on their reported NAVs. Of course, they're operating real estate companies, too, making this practice wholly inconsistent with their investment fundamentals.

Investors buying public REITs should have several key takeaways from this. First and foremost, publicly traded REITs are not traded with a relationship with their net asset value. Instead, they are priced by the operating performance of each share, which can be expressed through a variety of metrics at different levels of the business such as adjusted funds from operations per share, cash available for distribution per share, or even more unique metrics like net debt to EBITDA.

In a period of uncertainty or a recession, the operating performance of publicly traded REITs is likely to become more volatile than the transactional prices of the assets themselves. This has led to the extraordinary decline of REITs like Rexford Industrial (REXR) and Americold Realty (COLD), both of which are exposed to trade policies.

While their real estate remains top-notch from a long-term investment perspective, both companies have fallen sharply as investors anticipate the impact on the bottom line in coming quarters. I believe this combines with other investor behavior to generate the increasing volatility of public real estate.

Public REITs are more liquid than private REITs and far more liquid than commercial real estate. By this, I mean that selling shares of VNQ is a quicker process than submitting a redemption request in a private REIT with quarterly liquidity, such as BREIT. Selling these shares is also much faster, and considerably less expensive, than starting the disposition process for a commercial asset worth millions of dollars. This means that funds will generally look to their stack of public REITs first when making thematic decisions.

Right now, I would venture to guess many investors are looking to cut their exposure to real estate, which is typically considered a risk forward asset.

In a world where REITs offer a modest yield over the ten-year treasury yield, making the decision to reduce risk looks attractive with safe alternatives that offer a real return. This is yet again a reflection of Marks’ point that the pricing of liquid assets is a reflection of daily thinking around investments. When paired with commercial real estate which is relatively illiquid, it means that REIT pricing has a better chance of being misaligned with long-term fundamentals.

Conclusion

From my chair, volatility breeds opportunity in commercial real estate. While investors make decisions based on current events, the fundamentals of Class A commercial real estate remain unchanged over the course of decades. If we are willing to wait decades for an investment to pan out, buying in a down market when pricing is well-off of all-time highs could be a prudent decision. VNQ earns a Buy rating amidst a volatile real estate market.

data.title: UPS: Too Cheap To Ignore (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) stock has been bruised in the past year, with losses totaling over 35%. While technical weakness may continue for the stock, the valuation has become too cheap to ignore. In the below analysis, I have determined that most technicals remain highly bearish with the moving averages and the Ichimoku Clouds showing an undesirable outlook. There are some near term positive signs in the indicators and there was a bullish engulfing pattern recently. However, with the lack of other bottoming signals, further declines may occur. For the fundamentals, Q4 results were mixed and full year 2025 guidance was on the weak side for sure. However, the P/S ratio's contraction to multiyear lows seems to be overdone with revenue growth expected to remain at around average levels. The stock seems significantly undervalued, especially with its major discount versus the Industrials sector. While further technical weakness is likely from here, the stock seems too cheap to ignore currently, and so I believe a buy rating is warranted at this juncture.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

UPS is currently in a near term downtrend. This trend line began back in late January and last provided resistance in late March. However, the formation of a bullish engulfing pattern on the previous trading day could signal that there's a reversal for the stock. There are three additional resistance levels other than the downtrend line. The first is at around 109. This level was a downside gap early this month that occurred on the highest volume of the month thus far. This level was also supported back in late January and early February. The next resistance level is at around 122. This area was strongly supported back in December while being key resistance in early March. Lastly, there is distant resistance at about 134. The major gap down in January occurred at this level on by far the highest volume in the past six months. While there are no major support levels for UPS on this chart, the bullish engulfing pattern could act as some support, as a break below it would void the reversal signal.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has been below the 200-day SMA for the entirety of the past six months to show that long-term sentiment has been consistently bearish. The gap between the SMAs has continued to widen since late January and so bearish momentum is still increasing for the long term currently. For the EMAs, the latest crossover was a bearish one that occurred back in late January. The 9 day EMA failed to break above in March and instead bounced off the 21 day EMA as a sign of resilient bears. Currently, the gap between the EMAs is significant and so near term momentum is still bearish.

The stock gapped below the Bollinger Band's midline back in late January to show that a downtrend may be in its early stages. UPS was able to break back above for a brief period in early March, but ultimately, the stock slumped back below to show that a downtrend was indeed occurring. Recently, the stock has dipped below the lower band to show an oversold state, but the bounce on the previous trading day has moved the stock back within the bands. For the MACD, there was a bearish crossover in late March. The deviation between the lines grew substantially to reflect the major increase in bearish sentiment, but on the previous and current trading days, the histogram has turned bullish to show that near term sentiment has likely bottomed. However, the MACD still sits at six month lows, confirming the recent plunge in the stock. For RSI, it has rebounded from an extremely bearish reading of below 30 to a level of 31.97 currently. This shows a slight loosening of the bears' grasp but, nonetheless, they remain in solid control. Like the MACD, the RSI confirms the stock's declines with its own long-term low set just days ago. Lastly, for the stochastics, there was a true bullish crossover recently as the %K was able to break above the %D within the 20 zone. Furthermore, the %K seems to be breaking above 20 now as another sign that the bears are retreating at least for the near term.

Takeaway

Overall, I would say that the technical outlook, presented in these classic analysis tools, remains bearish. The stock remains in a downtrend, the moving averages demonstrate bearish momentum, while the MACD and RSI confirm the plunge in the stock. There are some positive near term signs, however, like the MACD histogram turning positive and the stochastics having a bullish crossover that show there are some near term reprieve for UPS. The bullish engulfing pattern could be a significant reversal signal for the long term, but with the lack of other bottoming signals, its reliability may be questioned until further notice.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the late October high as the starting point and the previous day's low as the end point, some potential price targets or resistance levels are shown above. The nearest level is 23.6% which is at around 104 for UBS. The next level could be an even more significant one. The 36.2% level sits just above 110, a round number. Furthermore, as identified in the charting analysis above, there is a resistance level quite near 110 as well. Therefore, that area could be major resistance for the stock if it's able to stage a major bounce.

Projection

Using the late October high again, the January low, and the retracement to just above 120, some support levels are show by the Fibonacci Projection in this chart. The stock broke beneath the 61.8% level on the third trading day this month and has since experienced resistance there due to the reversal in polarity. Due to the fact that the 61.8% level coincides with the 100 price level, this area's importance is amplified. The next level of support for UBS is the 100% level which is in the mid to high 80s price range and so the stock could have further to fall.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock has remained consistently below the cloud since the start of December and currently, it sits far below as a sign of strong general bearishness. The cloud itself has been bearish for the vast majority of the past six months. The cloud is not overly thick as of now, but moving in to May, with the deviation between the Leading Span A and Leading Span B having increased significantly, there could be heavy resistance for the stock in the near future. For near term momentum, the Conversion Line spent a brief period above the baseline in late March, but ultimately, there was a bearish crossover. Currently, the gap between these lines continues to expand to show that near term bearish momentum is still increasing. Lastly, despite the stock's bounce on the previous trading day, the Lagging Span remains far below UPS' levels in late February, confirming the downtrend. Looking back, there was a key sell signal back in late March. The cloud itself was bearish, the stock was well beneath the cloud, the Conversion Line and baseline had a bearish crossover, and the Lagging Span broke beneath the early February stock price. This was a sign of things to come for UPS stock that should not have been ignored.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

At the end of January, UPS released their earnings for 2024 Q4 and showed mixed results. They reported consolidated revenues of $25.3 billion, up from $24.9 billion in the prior year period. Their U.S. Domestic Segment and International Segment both saw YoY gains in revenue but Supply Chain Solutions weighed on results with its revenue decline. The decrease in revenue was attributed to the divestiture of Coyote. For EPS, the company reported an adjusted figure of $2.75, up 11.3% YoY. UPS had a mixed performance versus analyst expectations as revenues missed by $114.12 million while EPS beat by $0.22. As shown above, revenue growth saw QoQ declines and shows that the resurgence in the figure has stalled for the time being. Still, current levels of revenue growth are at above average levels compared to the past few years even though it's only near zero. Lastly, as you can see above, the operating margin of the business has shown both YoY and QoQ improvement. Operating margin is now at its highest level since 2023 Q2 and so the efficiency of the business seems to be on the right track. Overall, I would say these earnings results were a bit mixed. The financials as a whole seem resilient, but there is no question that growth has been underwhelming and remains at sluggish levels.

Guidance

UPS did not provide guidance specific to their 2025 Q1, but they did provide a brief full-year outlook. For the full year 2025, they are expected to see revenues of approximately $89.0 billion. For 2024, they had total revenues of $91.1 billion and so implied growth from this guidance is -2.3%. In 2023, revenues were reported to be $91.0 billion and so for 2024 they were able to generate growth of +0.11%, still better than the negative growth projected for this year. Overall, I believe it is clear that UPS is expecting a soft 2025 as the growth recovery is continuing to stall. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha currently has a C- Revision Grade for the company as 96.55% of FY1 Revisions have been downward in the past 90 days, showing that analysts expect weakness in their earnings moving forward.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

Despite the company's struggles as discussed above, I believe the stock is significantly undervalued at current levels. The P/S ratio has been on a multiyear downtrend and major contraction has occurred so far in 2025. The multiple is now at rock bottom levels, very near the three-year low set just days ago. As discussed above, revenue growth has remained above average despite the QoQ declines. In addition, while 2025 full-year guidance shows that revenue growth will dip back into negative territory, a -2.3% growth rate is still above the multiyear average, as you can see in the revenue growth chart above. While the outlook is definitely weaker than desired, the P/S ratio's contraction seems to be overdone and is an overreaction to the modestly worsening outlook for the company, Currently, the Industrials sector has a median P/S ratio of 1.36 and so UPS is at a 33.4% discount to peers. While the fundamentals are sluggish as of now, with the company being a key transport provider worldwide, I believe this discount is overly harsh. Seeking Alpha currently holds an A- Valuation Grade for UPS, confirming my evaluation.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, it was determined that most technicals remain bearish for UPS stock. There are some near term positive signs in the indicators but the bullish engulfing pattern is unable to convince me that the long-term bottom is in. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings were lackluster and full year 2025 guidance was overall soft. However, the P/S ratio's contraction shows that investors have overreacted to the weakening fundamentals, as revenue growth is actually projected to remain at around average levels relative to the past few years. Therefore, I believe the stock has become a buy with the valuation being too cheap to ignore. That said, further technical losses are possible and investors should keep that in mind.

data.title: Navigating Today's Volatile Market: A Stress-Free Approach
data.description:
Introduction:

The stock market is not only going through a volatile period, but it is also facing some very big unknowns. No one knows exactly how the tariff situation will unfold and what their precise impacts will be on inflation, interest rates, bond markets, and the overall economy. What is making it difficult is the unpredictability of President Trump's decisions. Until April 2nd, markets believed that tariff threats were mostly rhetoric and negotiation tactics. That all changed on the so-called “Liberation Day” with the announcement of unprecedented tariffs on almost the entire world, as it seemed no one was able to escape them. The trend towards globalization that has continued for so many decades has now suddenly been turned upside down. This also comes at a time when inflation still hasn't been fully contained and the interest rates are still quite high. Now, most people agree that the current trade imbalances and deficits are unsustainable for the U.S., and something needs to be done to correct the course. However, solutions will vary widely depending on who you ask. In fact, most real solutions will cause some pain and dislocation.

The market took a deep dive in the first two days after the tariff announcements, and the S&P500 touched the bear territory (20% below its most recent peak), while the Nasdaq had already been in the bear territory for some time. This past week, the market has been all over the place, often changing directions at midday based on hourly news.

Now, as we write this, most of the “reciprocal” tariffs have been paused for 90 days by President Trump, while the 10% base tariffs will continue. Also, the tariff on China has been upped to 125% (possibly 145%, including the 20% that was already there) in lieu of retaliatory tariffs. The new announcement took a lot of stress out of the system, at least initially, and the stock market immediately rallied by nearly 10% in a single day. All said and done, these uncertainties will continue for some time to come.

It is also true that the market weakness was apparent even before the tariff tantrum started. Part of the reason was obviously the fact that markets have been expensive to start with, especially some of the technology and growth stocks. There was some level of euphoria in the market, especially with Mag-7 and AI-related stocks. Some of the price corrections started back in February, immediately after China's DeepSeek announcement of their AI model, which was claimed to cost a fraction of the cost of their American counterparts. Though the accuracy of those claims is still in doubt, it forced the market to reassess the valuation multiples of some of the AI-leading stocks.

Three Types of Retail Investors:

We can generally put retail investors into three categories. Sure, we are only talking about long-term investors and not traders.

(I) The 401K passive investors: These are the folks who are in their prime working age (at least 10 years away from retirement) and contributing to their 401K accounts to save as well as to get the match from their employers. Most of their investments are in limited funds or ETFs made available by their benefit plans. These folks have really nothing to worry about and should just stay put with their plans and basically view the current situation as if nothing has changed. This is just day-to-day or short-term noise from the market that is best ignored. They should not make any short-term decisions based on fear or emotions.

(II) Active Investors: These are active (or somewhat active) investors who like to take charge of their investments. They probably have investments both in their 401Ks and other taxable accounts. They also tend to follow the market on a day-to-day basis, even if they are invested for the long term. These are the investors who are at the most risk, as they would be prone to making irrational decisions based on fear or emotions. When they see large losses in their accounts, they get the urge to do something. So, if you are already invested for the long term, it is best to stay put and not be swayed by the market. If you have a lot of cash sitting idle on the side, it will be good to keep a wish list of some sort ready and invest on a gradual basis whenever the market gives you another chance. For example, if you have $100,000 of cash to invest, we think it will be wise to make a plan to invest progressively with each successive market drop. However, we may caution that it is easier said than done.

When the market is 10% below its recent peak

Investing 10%

IF the market is 20% below its recent peak

Investing 20%

IF the market goes down further to 30% below the recent peak

Investing 25%

In an extreme situation of a recession or some kind of panic, if the market falls between 40-50%

Investing another 25%

We also recommend reading our other article that attempts to demonstrate how consistently buying low and selling high can build wealth.

(III) Investors who are retirees or less than 10 years away from retirement:

If you are already invested for the long term, with regular income coming, it is best to stay put with the plan in place. For those who are looking for an investment plan that is conservative, relatively safe, and can generate reasonable income, we will describe one such strategy below in the next section.

Strategy: The SWAN (Sleep Well At Night) Investing Strategy

The first priority of this strategy or the portfolio would be to provide a certain level of peace of mind, as the title suggests. But at the same time, it should provide excellent growth and some income. This strategy should be such that you don't need to worry too much about whether the market is going up, down, or sideways. The main emphasis here is that the ride should be much smoother, and drawdowns are much smaller when they do happen. Sure, there are always two sides to everything. In a raging bull market, this portfolio may underperform the S&P500 somewhat, but not by a whole lot.

Below, we outline a simple portfolio consisting of only five components, four of them with an allocation of 22.5% each, totaling 90%. The remaining 10% can be invested in one of the GOLD funds. This portfolio may not beat the S&P500 at all times but will grow consistently with nearly 30% lower drawdowns and potentially half the stress. Further, the portfolio provides many features: a broad diversification, a bet on the growth of the U.S. economy over time, and an inbuilt hedging mechanism to protect against severe downturns. Also, a small allocation of gold helps provide protection against inflation and currency fluctuations. Furthermore, it is a buy-and-hold portfolio and requires no active management. We provide two alternatives.

Table 3: SWAN Portfolio Strategy – Model 1

Table 3B: SWAN Portfolio Strategy – Income Model (Model 2)

Two of the four major components are highly popular dividend ETFs. These two ETFs are Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and Vanguard High Dividend Fund ETF (VYM). These two ETFs may have some overlap but are still at an acceptable level. Both of these ETFs are low-cost and have highly favorable performance records, with average yields of 3.5%.

Some of the top holdings of SCHD include Coca-Cola (KO), Verizon (VZ), Altria (MO), ConocoPhillips (COP), Lockheed Martin (LMT), PepsiCo (PEP), and Chevron (CVX).

Some of the top holdings of VYM include Broadcom (AVGO), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), ExxonMobil (XOM), Walmart (WMT), Proctor & Gamble (PG), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and Home Depot (HD).

The third component is Berkshire Hathaway stock (BRK.B) in Model-1, which is largely a proxy and a bet on the growth of the US economy; however, Berkshire provides no dividends or income. The long-term record of BRK is outstanding, and it seems we can't go wrong with it. At the same time, there is some uncertainty about the future performance of the company post its founder and legendary investor, Warren Buffett.

Model-2 replaces Berkshire Hathaway with JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), an ETF that holds most of the Nasdaq 100 stocks but also writes option calls using ELNs (Equity Linked Notes). The reason for this change is to elevate the income to a decent level. If you do not need income, the first model is likely more attractive.

The fourth major component (with an allocation of 22.5%) is the hedging component. For this component, investors could use the JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund (JHQAX) or its similar ETF version, the JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF (HELO). However, investing in the ETF is simpler and straightforward. We are using JHQAX for our back testing purposes as it has nearly 12 years of history, while HELO has less than two years. Please note that there are some minor differences between HELO and JHQAX, and their performance may not exactly be the same. These hedged equity funds aim to provide decent growth but also provide protection against large market downturns. They help investors to participate in equity market gains while mitigating risk in a declining market.

Here is the comparative action (HELO vs. SPY) during the tariff tantrum period from March to April 19th. You can see the difference in volatility.

These funds invest in a portfolio of U.S. large-cap stocks while employing a disciplined options strategy that seeks to reduce downside risk in falling markets. In some ways, HELO may be better, as it uses a laddered options strategy to provide protection. Both HELO and JHEQX share the same management team.

Chart-1:

The fifth and last component of the portfolio has a small allocation of 10% to one of the GOLD ETFs. It could be any form of GOLD-related fund /security, but not necessarily gold mining companies, as they come with higher risks. We are selecting GLD because it has a long history (for our back test), but one could invest in a similar fund, SPDR Gold MiniShares ETF (GLDM), for lower fees of only 0.10%.

We can also replace the GOLD ETF with some Gold Royalty companies, such as Franco-Nevada Corporation (FNV) or Royal Gold (RGLD). They currently provide about 1% yield.

Performance Testing:

Chart-1A: Comparison of the SWAN Model with the S&P500

Now, each of the SWAN models has underperformed the S&P500 slightly, but the ride is much smoother compared to the S&P500. The volatility is 30% less, and on a risk-adjusted returns basis, they have done very well.

Concluding Thoughts:

Our simple advice is to avoid fear and greed. Long-term investors need to stay put with their plans because most bear markets last only 12-24 months. We have also presented two relatively safe, conservative strategies with 30% less volatility and drawdowns for folks who are debating when and where to invest. As always, we welcome your comments.

data.title: Buy 9 'Safer' Dividend Dogs Of 23 April Barron's Better Bets Than T-Bills
data.description:
Foreword

While half of this collection of Barron’s Better Bets (BBB) is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny dividends, nine of the ten ‘lowest priced Dogs with the “Safest”dividends the BBB are ready to buy. April finds Altria (MO), Best Buy (BBY), Verizon (VZ), Conagra (CAG), U.S. Bancorp (USB), Regions Financial (RF), Kimco Realty (KIM), Kinder Morgan (KMI), and UDR (UDR) in the top ten, living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price.

The remaining one of the March “Safer” top ten, Simon Property (SPG) shows a price within 38% of meeting the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding its single share price.

With renewed downside market pressure of 38%, it will be possible for all ten of the top BBB “Safer” dividend dogs to become elite fair-priced dogs with their annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced January BBB Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the same nine as “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 32.86% To 53.48% Net Gains From Top-Ten BBB Dogs By April 2026

Six of ten top BBB Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top-ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this April, 2025 yield-based forecast for BBB dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 60% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to April 2026 were:

Best Buy was projected to net $534.77, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 42% greater than the market as a whole.

LyondellBasell Industries (LYB) was projected to net $505.64, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 7% greater than the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $491.52, based on the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% less than the market as a whole.

KeyCorp (KEY) was projected to net $490.73, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% greater than the market as a whole.

U.S. Bancorp was projected to net $453.09, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% underer the market as a whole.

ONEOK (OKE) was projected to net $413.19 based on the median of target price estimates from 19 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 21% over the market as a whole.

Truist Financial Corp (TFC) was projected to net $386.74 based on the median of target prices estimated by 19 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% less than the market as a whole.

Regions Financial was projected to net $374.21, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 1% over the market as a whole.

Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) was projected to net $359.15, based on the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 8% greater than the market as a whole.

Simon Property Group was projected to net $328.61, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 45% over the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 43.38% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten BBB stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 8% greater than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

April BBB Broker Targets

The April, 2025 BBB 23 By Yield

Actionable Conclusion: April Top Ten BBB Stocks By Yield Ranged 5.49% To 9.86% Per YCharts

Top ten April BBB Dogs occupied seven of eleven Morningstar sectors.

In first place, was the lone basic materials dog, LyondellBasell [1]. A lone health care dog placed second Pfizer[2].

Then, two consumer staple stalwarts, placed third, and tenth, Altria Group [3], and Conagra Brands [10]. A lone consumer discretionary stock placed fourth, Best Buy, [4].

Then, another loner from the communication services sector placed fifth, Verizon [5]. Thereafter, two financial stocks placed sixth, and eighth, KeyCorp [6], and Truist Financial [8].

Finally,Two real estate sector dogs took the seventh, and ninth positions: Realty Income (O) [7], and Simon Property Group [9], and completed the April Dogs of the BBB by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows the relative strengths of the top ten April BBB dogs by dividend and price as of market close 4/10/2025.

This month just one of the top-ten BBB dogs shows a less than ideal status because SPG share price exceeds the projected annual dividends from $1k invested.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by nine of the highest yield Dogs of the BBB.

LyondellBasell, Pfizer, Altria, Best Buy, Verizon, KeyCorp, Realty Income, Truist, and Conagra, in the top ten, plus U.S. Bancorp, Regions Financial, Kimco, Kinder Morgan, and UDR outside the top ten, all lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

Furthermore, four more, Bristol-Myers, Mid-America Apartments, ONEOK, and Simon Property showed prices within 40% of meeting that ideal goal.

BBB Prices per Dividend Dollar

The full chart above reveals twenty-three BBB dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock. The least expensive dog of the BBB is LyondellBasell at $10.14 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is Entergy at $33.45 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Five of Twenty-Three BBB Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Five BBB so infected are: KeyCorp, Truist, Federal Realty, Williams Companies (WMB), and Entergy (ETR). These are considered unsafe to buy. Furthermore their infection explains how the are missing from the IDEAL “safer” dividend dog reckoning.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, tariffs and uncertainty.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Dividend Splits Could Get All Ten BBB Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, nine in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those nine, already ideal, more top-yield stocks are within striking distance of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise or split their dividends. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 28.17% To 48.12% Top Ten BBB Upsides To April, 2025; (32) No Downsides Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 0.86% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 BBB Dogs As of April 10, 2026

Ten-top BBB dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten BBB dogs selected 4/10/25 by yield, represented seven of eleven sectors. (Energy, Industrials, Technology, and Utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield BBB Dogs (34) To Deliver 33.16% Vs. (35) 32.87% Net Gains by All Ten Come April 10, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten BBB Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 0.86% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The ninth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Best Buy, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 53.48%.

The five lowest-priced BBB top-yield dogs for April were: KeyCorp; Pfizer; Conagra; TTruist Financial; Verizon, with prices ranging from $13.74 to $42.92.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of April 10 were: Realty Income; LyondellBasell; Altria; Best Buy; Simon Property, with prices ranging from $54.15 to $147.69.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the market dies and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential tariff pronouncements add momentum into 2025 (with the exception of the few tech stocks) look for all of the top ten to fifteen BBB Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly!

Afterword

Buy 9 of 15 ‘Safer’ Dividend April Dogs of the BBB

Early April 2025, 15 of the 23 BBB were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields, and dividend yields from $1000 invested exceeding the price of a single share..

How BBB 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price” Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that seven dividends from $1K invested in nine of the top ten 'safer' dividend BBB dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of dividends from $1,000 invested being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those seven ideal dogs were Altria, Best Buy, Verizon, Conagra, US Bank, Regions Financial, and Kimco.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of only 38% could put all ten share prices of these ten “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or make stock splits to the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

BBB IDEAL “Safer” Dividends From Positive Return & Cash Flow Exceeding Dividend Yields

Adding the two IDEAL dividends from stocks outside the top ten BBB list produces the above list of nine.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher BBB 23 Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" BBB dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: EWZS: Brazilian Small Caps Safest Emerging Market Play
data.description:
Brazil Stocks Bottoming Out

Brazilian small-cap stocks have taken a beating in the past six months and may be headed for a further decline amid the new trade war tensions that kicked off this week.

Brazil is heavily exposed to the United States and China but, as past cycles have shown, it may be able to benefit from this cycle in some ways. If commodity prices take off, Brazil is also positioned to benefit as a significant commodity exporter. It is best to not overlook Brazil, which is currently one of the cheapest emerging markets.

Growth will likely come in a bit slower in 2025 and 2026, but the market already appears to be priced for the worst at the moment. Despite being a top 5 MSCI Emerging Markets constituent, the market still trades at a 30% discount to this index.

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF (NASDAQ:EWZS) has underperformed general Brazilian ETFs but has also outperformed its small-cap peer ETF. 2025 looks like a good time to continue accumulating, especially after any trade war related sell-offs. The market already appears priced for the worse.

Economic Outlook

Brazil's economic growth will likely fall below 3% this year, below its historical average, due to global economic tensions. After the next few months, Brazil will be positioned to consider smaller rate cuts to help create stronger growth in 2026.

Growth is on a downturn: Brazil's average growth has been around 3% during the past three years, and recently grew by 3.4% last year. However, the combination of higher interest rates, trade war risks, and other global macro tensions will likely bring its economic growth below 3% in the next two years.

Weak Currency and Inflation: The combination of the country's weaker currency and rebound in inflation forced the Central Bank to reverse its policy and begin hiking rates again towards the end of 2024.

Rate Cut Outlook: Brazil's central bank has noted that it may step in to cut interest rates during the second half of this year. Even though inflation has crept up this year, it still has remained below 5.1% every month for the last 12 months. 2026 may be much more favorable, according to projections from BCB.

China and US Impact: Following the previous trade war, there was a significant uptick in Brazil's exports to China during the following five months. In the long run, its exports to China have nearly doubled since 2017.

Brazil's Exports to China

Similarly, Brazil's exports to the United States have also risen steadily since 2017. Given that Trump has been willing to discuss free trade agreements with major partners, like Argentina, for example, Brazil could come out ahead here by staying close to China and the United States this year. These markets account for nearly 40% of its exports though, so any economic shocks in these markets would be detrimental for Brazil.

Value Investing in Small Caps

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF invests in 72 companies and charges a 0.59% management fee. This ETF is a stellar value vehicle that trades at a substantial discount to the MSCI Brazil Index. It is well diversified, with the top 10 holdings only accounting for 34% of its assets.

This ETF trades at around 7.6x earnings and trades slightly below book value. The assets of this ETF currently exceed $100 million, so there is not a strong risk of delisting, as seen with other smaller ETFs with AUM below $40 million. Moreover, Brazil is still the 5th largest MSCI EM Index constituent and also accounts for more than 60% of the MSCI Emerging Market Latin America Index.

MSCI EM Latin America Index

Sector Exposure: Since I last covered this ETF in 2023, some of the sector weights have changed. The consumer themes weighting rose by around 3 percentage points, industrials dropped by over 10 pp, and financials rose by around 11 pp.

This looks like a very favorable way to approach the market, especially since this ETF trades at a substantial discount to Brazilian large caps. While it has underperformed traditional Brazil ETFs, this ETF has outperformed the VanEck Brazil Small-Cap ETF (BRF) by around 15 percentage points in the last five years.

As seen in this chart, this ETF was a major outperformer following 2020 when emerging market stocks bottomed out, so this trend could repeat when emerging markets rally again.

Risks and Thoughts to Note

The next couple of months will be bumpy for emerging markets, so finding the perfect entry point is nearly impossible.

Emerging markets, particularly China, have been hit harder than the S&P 500 in the past five days.

Trade War: We are certainly not out of the clear in terms of tariffs, as tensions have only been escalating recently with US partners like China and Canada. Brazil's export structure is not very diversified, as China and the USA alone account for circa 40% of its total exports.

Inflation: A lot of inflation projections were made with trade war risks in mind, but may have underestimated the current tensions. Brazil is less exposed because it only faces the minimum tariff charge. However, other inflationary risks, such as tensions in the Middle East, may surprise to the upside this year. Brazil's Central Bank has taken a very cautious approach to rate cuts, even with inflation improving in 2024, and will likely hold back for a couple more months at least.

Peers: EM Latin American markets are also very cheap, only trading at a slight premium to Brazil. If free trade agreements go through with Argentina and the USA, this market could absorb capital flows. Other markets like Colombia are also extremely undervalued.

Other Risks: Brazil's currency was one of the worst performing major currencies in 2024, declining by around 20%. The country's external debt levels are still relatively high, as the country has had to take on a lot of new debt since Covid.

Brazil's External Debt

Valuation Appeal: The main appeal of Brazil is also related to the lack of compelling narratives in other emerging markets. Other larger Asian emerging markets also stand to suffer from trade war risks, yet these markets are much more expensive. The MSCI Asia AC Index trades at 15x earnings, a notable premium to Brazil and other South American markets.

At the moment, Brazil can be characterized as a market with acceptable risks and high return potential. It is not directly exposed like China yet is priced at a steal. In particular, this ETF trades at a significant discount to the broader market in Brazil.

It makes sense to take advantage of any pullbacks in Q2 2025 to gain exposure to Brazil. Investors will be hard-pressed to find another emerging market with a better risk reward potential.

data.title: Ultra High Conviction: My #1 Defensive Place To Invest Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

Markets are in turmoil.

In the past two weeks, we had:

  • The S&P 500's third-best day since World War II, as a result of a 90-day tariff pause for all nations except China.
  • The largest Cboe Volatility Index decline in history, as it fell by more than 30% on the same day the market had its third-best day.
  • The market's fifth-worst two-day drop in early April, when 'Liberation Day' kicked off a new wave of tariffs.
  • The Dow Jones had its biggest four-day losing streak ever. However, this needs to be taken with a grain of salt, as this is measured in points instead of percentage points. But still, it sounds catchy.
  • The worst week in years was followed by the best week since 2023.

Things have become so volatile that it's almost impossible for me to write long articles, as the moment I'm halfway, some new headline drops that cause the market to move a few percent.

However, the worst thing is that markets have started to exhibit a lot of fear.

By that, I mean that 'normal' trading patterns have broken down.

For example, we're seeing both one of the steepest declines in the dollar index and one of the steepest increases in longer-term bond yields.

The surge in rates happens despite a highly favorable inflation number on Thursday and the positive 90-day tariff pause news. It also did not keep the dollar from plummeting, which triggered another rally in the price of gold.

Although I disagree with everyone who will make the case that America is losing its reserve currency status, I agree with people who point out that all of this screams elevated instability:

Treasuries and the dollar get their strength from “the world’s perception of the competence of American fiscal and monetary management and the solidity of American political and financial institutions,” said Jim Grant, founder of Grant’s Interest Rate Observer, a widely followed financial newsletter. “Possibly, the world is reconsidering.” - Bloomberg

In fact, U.S. treasuries had their worst performance relative to German bunds in history, as the chart below shows. That's quite something, as Germany itself is dealing with structural issues and is heavily reliant on government spending.

And then there's oil, which is has fallen below $60, as the market is pricing in much slower economic growth on a global scale, as globalization was a major driver of international prosperity - even if it was a net negative for industrial regions like the American Rust Belt or the German Ruhrgebiet, among many others.

Moreover, earnings estimates are coming down. Even before 'Liberation Day,' earnings estimates for the S&P 500 were adjusted at a slightly above-average rate, as FactSet data shows.

During the first quarter, analysts lowered EPS estimates by a larger margin compared to the three most recent averages. The Q1 bottom-up EPS estimate (which is an aggregation of the median EPS estimates for Q1 for all the companies in the index) decreased by 4.2% (to $60.23 from $62.89) from December 31 to March 31. - FactSet

As earnings season is about to shift into a higher gear in the upcoming two weeks, we'll get a lot more data and valuable comments from executives who will comment on tariffs, consumer sentiment, inflation, artificial intelligence, and everything else you can think of.

Having said all of this, people asked me what my favorite defensive industry is for (dividend) investors, in light of global uncertainty.

Like all "if you could pick just one" questions, it took me a few days to figure it out until I realized my favorite defensive industry is part of a cyclical sector.

Hence, in the second part, I will tell you my favorite industry for the next few years and the three stocks that stand out to me.

Now, let's dive in!

It's All About One Word & One Place That Benefits

"Uncertainty."

So far, that's the theme of 2025, as we are dealing with a mix of tariff fears, economic growth worries, inflation uncertainty, and other factors.

To make matters worse, all of these factors are somewhat related and influencing each other, as without sticky inflation and somewhat 'unfortunate choices' from the prior administration, the U.S. would not have to refinance trillions in debt at what are currently very unfavorable rates.

In light of the many challenges, I wrote articles like "Buckle Up Buttercup [...]" in December of 2024, as I believe the next few years are not going to be as comfortable as the past few years, which had rather low volatility and elevated returns, as the JPMorgan chart below shows quite well (as of March 31).

With that said, I had many sectors/industries in mind.

  • Utilities are defensive, they enjoy secular growth from AI-related power demand, they tend to have decent dividends, and overall, healthy balance sheets. However, to me, they are too boring. For income, they may be interesting, but that's it.
  • Real estate is one of my favorite sectors, as it has been beaten down by rising rates. A lot of REITs offer terrific dividends and defensive business models, as the 'big guys' have all kept strong occupancy rates in periods like the Great Financial Crisis. However, due to interest rate risks, it's not my number on sector.
  • I also thought of making energy the star of this article. It's one of the cheapest places to put money due to the decline in oil prices. It is also home to some very efficient companies with low breakeven prices and consistent dividend growth (even during recessions). However, due to the cyclical nature of these stocks and elevated volatility, they are not number one. Also, they are not defensive, which is the number one criterion for this article.
  • Consumer staples are another area I like. Even the strongest players like PepsiCo (PEP), which I own, have suffered due to fears of declining pricing power. However, as much as I like this industry, the risks of prolonged uncertainty and tariff fears keep it from becoming my number one sector.

As we can see below, the three defensive sectors I just mentioned all outperformed the S&P 500 year-to-date (excluding dividends). Energy (not defensive) started underperforming the market due to OPEC's decision to increase output, which could not have come at a worse time.

While I like various stocks in the sectors I just mentioned, this article is about a different sector, or at least about a quite unique industry in a cyclical sector.

I'm talking about the financial exchange & data industry, which accounts for roughly half of the iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchange ETF (IAI).

As we can see below, the IAI has underperformed the S&P 500 this year, after outperforming the market over the past ten years (256% vs. 197%, including dividends). This makes sense, as the other half of the ETF consists of investment banks, which are hyper cyclical.

The industry I am talking about is much stronger.

Stock exchange operators own the plumbing of the (global) financial system. They operate the exchanges we need to trade, which allows them to make money on every trade we execute, the more, the better.

On top of that, there are other (related) factors that make them great:

  • Higher demand for hedging products: During volatile markets, investors and institutions use derivatives like options and futures to manage risk. The higher the uncertainty, the more corporations and other investors tend to use hedging products.
  • Elevated market data sales: Volatile periods tend to draw more attention to real-time market data, as traders and institutions need insights, which leads to higher subscription and access fees for exchange-provided data. Moreover, the more people trade, the more data exchange companies can generate. This means the bigger they become, the more 'size' becomes a competitive advantage.
  • Clearing and settlement activity: Higher trading during periods of volatility increases the need for clearing services, which ensure trades are completed. Without reliable trade execution, we would run into serious issues. While recent volatility wasn't fun, even when markets crashed more than 6% in a single day, the 'system' worked.
  • Scalability: Because exchanges are almost entirely electronic (we don't have to fly to Chicago to bet on corn futures), it is very easy for these companies to scale operations, which often comes with very high margin benefits and inflation protection. It also helps that inflation surges often come with higher volatility.

Simply put, the higher the volatility and uncertainty, the more these companies benefit.

The 3 Companies Worth Buying & Watching

There are three stock exchange operators that dominate the landscape:

  • CME Group (CME)
  • Intercontinental Exchange (ICE)
  • Cboe Global Markets (CBOE)

There are also smaller players, including Coinbase Global (COIN) for crypto and MarketAxess Holdings (MKTX), which is an emerging player for bond futures.

However, for now, I focus on the three biggest players, which I prefer anyway, as they have less 'size' and 'niche' risks like COIN and MKTX.

The interesting thing is that each of these companies brings something very special to the table.

CME Group is a pure-play exchange operator and the largest derivatives marketplace on the face of the earth, with portfolio exchanges like the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (where it got its name), Chicago Board of Trade, NYMEX, and COMEX.

On its exchanges, investors trade some of the most liquid assets, including the most liquid futures in the world, which is the S&P 500 e-mini. It also offers a wide range of agricultural, interest rate, energy, metal, and forex futures and options, providing investors and corporate players with almost everything they need to get the job done.

Additionally, it is consistently adding new products, including Bitcoin futures, lithium, and other niche futures for very specific purposes.

This venture into new areas is a great way to benefit from emerging trends without having to invest a substantial amount, which is the asset-light benefit I just briefly mentioned.

With regard to the current environment, in 1Q25 (before the current turmoil started), average daily volumes were up 13%, which is a terrific result, likely followed by a much higher number for the second quarter.

Intercontinental Exchange is different. The company, which owns the New York Stock Exchange, has made the decision to diversify. Currently, it generates roughly half of its money from exchange operations, with most revenue coming from energy and financial futures. These futures include targeted financial products for specific purposes.

For example, it has Midland WTI futures for Permian (Midland) oil production, Brent Crude futures, European natural gas futures (including LNG), Dubai Murban futures, and many other products. Since 2019, this energy business has grown by 14% per year.

Meanwhile, the other half of its revenue comes from mortgage technologies and fixed income/data services. Due to aggressive M&A, the company now owns a big part of the mortgage 'supply chain', which includes real estate data tracking, lead generation, underwriting, closing, loan trading, and loan servicing, among many other things.

As a result, despite being an exchange operator, roughly half of its revenue is recurring, which adds a layer of safety. And while its mortgage business is cyclical, it has strong secular growth due to mortgage process modernization.

Then there's Cboe.

Cboe Global Markets operates as an exchange for options, equities, futures, and foreign exchange, with a core focus on the Chicago Board Options Exchange, which is where it got its name.

Unlike CME and ICE, its business model revolves around supporting trading in equity index options (like SPX and VIX), single-stock options, and exchange-traded products.

Revenue streams include transaction fees, clearing fees, market data subscriptions, and proprietary index licensing. This includes the Cboe Volatility Index, which I have brought up almost daily in recent weeks.

Cboe also operates equities exchanges in the U.S., Europe, and Canada, diversifying its footprint while benefiting from strong international demand for American financial equities.

Or to put it differently, Cboe is an option-focused company with growth opportunities in areas that have gone overlooked by many other players in recent years.

Especially after the pandemic, when people became more speculative, Cboe hit it out of the park, supported by new products like 0DTE (same-day expiration) options.

Personally, I bought CME Group because I loved its pure-play futures/options business model and its special dividends.

With regard to dividends, both ICE and CBOE pay steadily rising quarterly dividends.

ICE, which yields 1.2%, has a payout ratio of just 30% and a five-year CAGR of 10.2%. It has grown its dividend every year for more than a decade, supported by aggressive buybacks in years without M&A-related deleveraging.

Meanwhile, CBOE shares also yield 1.2% with a payout ratio of 27% and a five-year CAGR of 12%. The company has hiked its dividend every single year for 14 years. Now, it has a bigger focus on organic growth (without M&A), which will likely support elevated dividend growth going forward.

CME Group also pays four quarterly dividends.

However, it also pays one annual special dividend, which usually pushes the total cash payout ratio to 100%. CME simply doesn't need a lot of cash to run the business. And, as it has a double A-range credit rating, it does not need to take care of its balance sheet.

So, almost every penny of free cash flow goes to shareholders.

Currently, I estimate that the company will pay close to 5% on an annualized basis. The base dividend accounts for roughly half of that number. In February, the base dividend was hiked by 8.7%.

Having said all of this, over the past ten years, an equal-weight basket of these three stocks has returned 16.9% per year, beating the 13.4% annual return of the S&P 500 by a very decent margin.

Even better, the standard deviation was barely higher at 16.7%, which is a fantastic accomplishment, as we are comparing just three stocks to a diversified portfolio of 500 stocks.

Hence, the risk-adjusted return of these three stocks was much better than the market, as the Sharpe and Sortino Ratios show.

Also, and this matters most, the three stocks ignored the recent sell-off, as we can see below.

So, what about their valuations?

Let's start with the bad news.

Cboe isn't cheap anymore. Currently, it trades at 24x earnings. While that's just slightly above its long-term average of 23.0x, it would require more than 8% annual EPS growth to produce an annual return of 10% or more.

While current expectations are for 8% EPS growth in 2025 and 2026 (FactSet), it's not a perfect risk/reward, especially because the current sell-off has produced some great bargains in other areas.

In my view, Cboe is a Hold.

ICE is a Buy.

The company trades at 24.8x earnings, in line with its long-term average. Analysts expect 11-12% annual EPS growth. Especially if rates come down a bit, I think ICE could benefit from long-term elevated volatility and secular growth in mortgages.

CME Group is a Buy as well.

Currently, the stock trades at 24.9x earnings. Its long-term average is 27.1x. While that may seem elevated, please note that it has the fourth-highest profit margins of the S&P 500 and a 100% payout.

These are high-quality earnings, as a lot of it ends up in shareholders' pockets instead of reinvestments.

When adding mid-single-digit annual EPS growth (I believe it will be much higher), we get an outlook of more than 12% annual total returns, similar to ICE.

All things considered, I love the stock exchange industry.

I own CME, and I'm looking to buy ICE as well.

Going forward, I truly believe these are the perfect assets to own for prolonged volatility and uncertainty.

If CME comes down a bit, I'll substantially expand my investment, likely making it one of my largest holdings.

Takeaway

Markets are on edge, with wild swings in stocks, bonds, oil, and currencies that make it hard to write an article before the next headline pops up.

While many defensive sectors like utilities, real estate, and staples have their benefits, I have realized my favorite defensive industry right now isn't in a defensive sector at all.

It's the financial exchanges industry, which includes companies like CME, ICE, and CBOE.

These are the behind-the-scenes operators of the global financial system, and the more uncertainty we face, the more they tend to thrive.

Higher volatility means more trading, more demand for data, and more hedging activity, which feeds directly into their income statements.

In a world where the only constant seems to be elevated uncertainty, these scalable, margin-rich businesses look like great places to be, with consistently rising income on top.

I don't think it gets much better than this, especially as I expect volatility to remain above-average on a longer-term basis.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is a sudden drop in market volatility. If uncertainty fades and trading volumes decline, demand for derivatives and market data could weaken.

If we were to get a longer-term market rebound, these stocks could temporarily underperform.

Also, general recession and market risks apply. While these three stocks have outperformed the market, a steep bear market and/or recession can cause (temporary) capital losses.
